mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 3 Series 316i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 316i engine typically costs £800–£3,800 supply only depending on which generation you have (M40/M43 engines are cheaper; N13/B38 engines are more expensive). Fitting adds £800–£1,400. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 3 Series 316i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which generation your 316i has. The M40 and M43 engines (E30/E36) are simple SOHC 8V units that are generally robust. The N42/N46 (E46) introduced VALVETRONIC but brought timing chain issues. The N13 (F30) is efficient but has a well‑documented timing chain vulnerability. The B38 (G20) is the most refined and reliable of the family.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 3 Series 316i?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most serious issue is timing chain tensioner wear on N42/N46 and N13 engines – the plastic guides can fail, causing chain stretch and potential catastrophic engine damage. Other common problems include VANOS/VALVETRONIC system issues, oil leaks (valve cover, oil filter housing), and HPFP failure on N43/N13 engines.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a 316i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £3,000–£10,000 316i, a £1,400–£3,800 recon is 15–40% of the car's value – often worth it. If the timing chain has snapped and caused valve damage, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my 3 Series's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 316i badge covers nine different engine variants across six generations. Check the engine code stamp on the engine block and your V5C. As a rule of thumb: E30/E36 (1987–1998) = M40/M43; E46 (1998–2005) = M43/N42/N46; E90 (2005–2011) = N45/N43; F30 (2012–2016) = N13; G20 (2019–present) = B38.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a 316i engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – timing chain tensioner wear (if caught early), VANOS/VALVETRONIC issues, oil leaks, HPFP failure – are repairable. However, if the timing chain has snapped, the engine is an interference design – pistons will strike valves. In this case, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the 316i pricing across generations. The older M40/M43 engines are cheaper to replace (£800–£2,200); the newer N13 and B38 engines command a premium (£1,800–£3,800). Older generations: see the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (N42/N46, N45/N43, N13)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic whine from the engine, engine warning light, loss of power, potential catastrophic failure — especially prevalent on N42/N46 and N13 engines.
problems.replacementCost: £1,600–£3,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (if chain snaps and damages the engine).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear a rattle or whine on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The N42/N46 and N13 engines are particularly vulnerable to timing chain issues. The plastic timing chain guides can become brittle and fail, causing chain stretch or snap. On the N13 (F30), this is the defining weakness of the engine. On the N42/N46 (E46), the timing chain tensioner is a known weak point. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement at £800–£2,000 is far cheaper than a £1,600–£3,500 engine replacement. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the timing system inspected immediately.

problems.title: VALVETRONIC / VANOS System Issues (N42/N46, N43)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, loss of power, hesitation on acceleration, engine warning light, poor fuel economy.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if engine warning light appears.

problems.recommendation: The N42/N46 and N43 engines feature BMW's VALVETRONIC variable valve lift and VANOS variable valve timing systems. These systems are sophisticated and effective but can develop issues. Solenoids can become clogged with oil sludge, and the VALVETRONIC motor can wear. Regular oil changes (using the correct BMW LL‑spec oil) are the best preventative measure. If the engine warning light appears with VANOS or VALVETRONIC codes, have the system diagnosed promptly.

problems.title: Oil Leaks — Valve Cover & Oil Filter Housing (N42/N46, N45/N43)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak from the top or front of the engine.
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 [PLACEHOLDER] (valve cover gasket or oil filter housing gasket replacement).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored oil loss can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected.

problems.recommendation: The N42/N46 and N45/N43 engines are known for oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing gasket. If the oil filter housing gasket fails, oil can leak onto the serpentine belt, causing it to slip or break — which can then be ingested into the engine through the front crankshaft seal, causing catastrophic failure. If you notice oil spots under your 316i or a burning oil smell, investigate immediately. A £300–£800 gasket replacement is far cheaper than the consequences of a snapped belt.

problems.title: High‑Pressure Fuel Pump (HPFP) Failure (N43, N13)
problems.symptoms: Long cranking on start (hot or cold), loss of power, engine stalling, engine warning light, reduced fuel economy.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if hard starting or power loss occurs.

problems.recommendation: The N43 (E90) and N13 (F30) use direct fuel injection with a high‑pressure fuel pump that can fail. Symptoms include long cranking times, particularly when hot, and loss of power. If the HPFP fails, the engine may run lean, causing misfires and potentially damaging the catalytic converter. Regular fuel filter changes and using high‑quality fuel can help prolong pump life, but HPFP replacement is a common maintenance item on these engines.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW 3 Series 316i — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — cheaper than engine replacement
Full engine replacement — valve damage is almost certain
Worth fixing — usually a solenoid or motor replacement
Worth fixing on any 316i worth keeping
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This BMW 3 Series 316i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full BMW service history with documented oil changes (every 10,000–12,000 miles)
* Timing chain inspected and any work documented (especially N42/N46/N13)
* Evidence of premium fuel use (recommended for direct‑injection engines)
* A well‑maintained example with lower mileage
avoidIf: * Any sign of cold‑start rattle — this is the defining weakness of later engines
* No evidence of regular oil changes — these engines are intolerant of neglect
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis
* Poor service history — these engines demand strict maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW 316i badge has spanned over 30 years and six generations of the 3 Series, from the simple M40 engine of the E30 to the modern B38 of the G20. The earlier M40 and M43 engines are robust and relatively simple, though ageing. The N42/N46 introduced VALVETRONIC but brought timing chain issues. The N13 (F30) is efficient and peppy but has a well‑documented timing chain vulnerability. Buy on service history above all else — a vehicle with documented timing chain maintenance and regular oil changes is a safe bet; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare 3 Series 316i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 3 Series 316i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any rattle or whine? (timing chain warning — stop immediately)
2. Service History — Regular oil changes (10,000–12,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
3. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the valve cover and oil filter housing
4. Running Quality — Any rough idle, hesitation, or power loss? (VANOS/VALVETRONIC)
5. Hot Start — Does it start cleanly when warm? (long cranking = HPFP warning)
6. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any timing, VANOS, or fuel pressure codes?
7. Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.
